title: Things to Do in Manuel Antonio: The Complete Activity Guide for 2026
excerpt: Manuel Antonio packs more diversity into its 16 square kilometers than almost any other Costa Rica destination. This guide moves beyond the national park to cover the full range of activities available in the area — from canopy tours and kayak adventures to sunset cruises and the town's food scene.
content:
Things to Do in Manuel Antonio: The Complete Activity Guide for 2026

Most visitors arrive in Manuel Antonio with the national park at the top of their list, and the park deserves that priority. But once you have walked the Punta Catedral trail, swum at Playa Espadilla, and watched the squirrel monkeys for an afternoon, the question becomes what else the area offers. The answer, it turns out, is quite a lot — Manuel Antonio and the surrounding Quepos area have developed a broad enough range of activities that a week here does not exhaust the options.

The National Park — Beyond the Basics

The Manuel Antonio National Park entry is covered in detail in our dedicated wildlife guide, but worth emphasizing here that the park experience changes substantially depending on how you approach it. The morning entry slot, arriving at opening time, produces a different park than the afternoon slot — more active wildlife, cleaner trails, fewer other visitors on the main circuits. If photography is your priority, the morning slot is the only practical choice.

The park's less-visited mangrove channels at the eastern edge of the property offer a paddling experience quite different from the main beach area. Kayak rentals are available in Quepos, and a self-guided paddle through the mangroves in the early morning produces encounters with birds and marine life that most park visitors never see.

Canopy Tours and Zipline Experiences

The forested hills above Manuel Antonio and Quepos support several zipline and canopy tour operations that offer a perspective on the forest that the park trails do not. Moving through the canopy at height — rather than from the trail below — reveals a different layer of the forest ecosystem and, from a photography standpoint, provides elevated angles over the canopy toward the ocean that are otherwise difficult to access.

The best canopy operation in the area combines zipline platforms with a skywalk element — a hanging bridges section that lets participants stop and observe rather than simply transit. For photography, the skywalk section is the more useful component, as it allows time to compose images from elevation without the wind and movement that zipline riding introduces.

Sunset Cruises and Sailing

The Quepos marina hosts several sailing and sunset cruise operators that depart in the late afternoon for a cruise along the coast toward Manuel Antonio. The coastal geology from the water — the steep forested slopes dropping to the sea, the headlands, the beaches accessible only from the ocean — is a different experience from the land perspective, and the late-afternoon light on the water produces conditions that are almost always more flattering than midday.

Some of the sailing operators include snorkeling stops at the reef areas off the coast, where the water clarity, while not Caribbean-standard, is sufficient for a reasonable underwater experience. The combination of sailing, snorkeling, and sunset makes this one of the most complete half-day activities available in the area.

Sport Fishing in Quepos

Quepos has a long history as a sport fishing port, and the offshore waters here are known for sailfish, mahi-mahi (dorado), and marlin. Full-day and half-day charters operate from the Quepos marina and represent one of the more established tourism activities in the area. Even non-anglers find the offshore experience worthwhile — the open ocean perspective, the chance of whale sightings during migration season, and the quality of the light on the water in the late afternoon all contribute to a memorable experience.

Photography from a sport fishing boat presents challenges — the boat moves, the light changes rapidly, and saltwater spray is a constant concern for gear. A telephoto zoom in the 100-400mm range handles the wildlife you might encounter (dolphins, sea turtles, whales during season), and a wide-angle or standard zoom captures the seascape when conditions allow. A polarizing filter is essential for cutting glare off the water surface.

Kayaking and Paddleboarding

The calm-water options around Manuel Antonio include the estuary at the north end of Playa Espadilla, the mangrove channels accessible from the park boundary, and the more sheltered bay areas near the villages of Matapalo and Portalon to the south. SUP (stand-up paddleboard) rentals are available in town and the calm early morning conditions in the bay are ideal for a paddle that doubles as wildlife viewing.

The Manuel Antonio estuary is particularly productive in the early morning, when the mangroves are active with birds and the water surface is still calm. Paddling quietly into the channels produces encounters with herons, kingfishers, and occasionally the raccoons that move between the mangroves and the beach.

Horseback Riding in the Hills

Several operations offer horseback riding in the hills above Manuel Antonio, through a landscape of cattle farms, forest patches, and viewpoints over the Pacific. The combination of terrain diversity and the Pacific panorama from the ridge makes this a productive half-day activity that also offers a different perspective on the area's rural character. Most operations cater to a range of riding abilities and include a stop at a waterfall or swimming hole along the route.

The Quepos Town and Food Scene

The town of Quepos deserves more than a passing visit. The Malecon — the waterfront promenade — is a pleasant evening walk and the spot where local families gather in the early evening. The covered market near the bus station is one of the most authentic food markets in the Manuel Antonio area, serving working-class Costa Rican food at prices that bear no relation to the tourist restaurants along the main strip. The ceviche at the market's food stalls is consistently excellent.

The restaurant scene in both Quepos and the beach road that connects it to Manuel Antonio has improved substantially over the years. International cuisine is well-represented — Italian, Japanese, contemporary Central American — alongside the seafood-focused restaurants that take advantage of the local fishing fleet's daily catch. The beach road in particular has accumulated enough dining options that the quality and variety is now genuinely competitive.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many days do I need for Manuel Antonio?

Two full days covers the essentials — the national park and either a sailing cruise or canopy tour. Three days allows for the addition of sport fishing, the mangrove kayaking, and time to explore Quepos at a relaxed pace. Four days starts to cover the secondary activities without feeling rushed.

Is Manuel Antonio good for non-wildlife activities?

Yes. The sailing and sunset cruises, sport fishing, kayaking, and paddleboarding are all strong activities that do not depend on wildlife sightings. The canopy tour and waterfall horseback rides are solid options regardless of interest in wildlife. The town's food scene is a genuine asset often overlooked by visitors focused entirely on the park.

What is the best activity for families with children?

The sailing cruise is the most reliably enjoyable family activity — the combination of sailing, swimming, and sunset works across age groups. The zipline and canopy tours are popular with children who are old enough to follow safety instructions. Kayaking the mangroves in the early morning is quieter and works for families with older children who can manage the paddling.

Are the adventure activities in Manuel Antonio safe?

The zipline and canopy operations are generally well-managed with good safety records, but it is worth verifying that the operator you choose has current safety certifications and well-maintained equipment. Sport fishing charters should hold current licensing. For any water-based activity, check weather conditions before departing and do not proceed if conditions seem questionable.

What is the best time of day for activities in Manuel Antonio?

Morning for the national park, wildlife viewing, and mangrove kayaking. Afternoon for sailing cruises and sunset photography. The midday hours from 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM are generally the least productive for wildlife and the most likely to produce uncomfortable heat — a good time for lunch, rest, or indoor activities.
